title: Superannuation: AusSuper tips strong year after 9.8pc return
full_text:
AustralianSuper's chief investment officer says the superannuation giant is well-positioned for another year of strong returns as the artificial intelligence boom broadens out from large US technology companies to other winners, after the fund's balanced option achieved a 9.77 per cent return in the 2025-26 financial year.
Shaun Manuell, the head of investments at Australia's largest retirement fund, said listed shares fuelled returns over the period, and the move to allocate more members' funds to US and offshore equities was its best decision.
"Equities really stole the show," Manuell said in his first interview since starting in the top job on Wednesday. "The call to be overweight international equities vis-a-vis domestic equities paid off and was the main driver in terms of relative and absolute performance."
Manuell said the market was in the midst of a phase of "rational exuberance", with money flocking to companies with AI exposure, and the fund had benefited from its willingness to actively choose investments rather than passively invest members' money in indices.
However, he said he was worried about the valuations of over-geared assets in a rising interest rate environment and was watching for signs of excessive exuberance that could lead to a market correction.
"We'll be very judicious when we look at things," he said. "The underwriting discipline is essential for us; we are going to buy things, but we're also cognisant of where we are with listed markets and market sentiment."
The 9.77 per cent return for AusSuper's balanced fund means it has delivered 8.47 per cent a year on average over the past decade. Its higher-risk growth fund returned 11.58 per cent in the financial year. In comparison, arch-rival Australian Retirement Trust's high-growth option returned 9.21 per cent, and its balanced pool 7.91 per cent.
High-growth funds take more risk and have higher return targets than balanced funds.
CBUS and Rest, two other big industry funds, also released their FY26 returns on Friday. CBUS' growth fund made 10.06 per cent for members, and Rest's returned 9.81 per cent.
Big retail fund MLC's high-growth option returned 10.2 per cent. The major funds' returns are consistent with their 10-year averages.
CBUS chief investment officer Leigh Gavin said his fund's exposure to data centres and other property helped its performance. Rest chief investment officer Michael Clancy said its private-equity investments were a "particularly strong contributor". MLC's Dan Farmer warned of another volatile year ahead.
The results were largely in line with the funds' 10-year averages.
Manuell said he was starting the new year mildly overweight in global equities but did not expect a repeat of 2025-26, where the MSCI World Index returned 22 per cent in Australian dollar terms.
He said US equity markets, by far the largest globally, had recorded outsized returns over the past three years, thanks to the AI investment, but would slow at some point. The biggest winners in FY26 included US and Korean-listed chipmakers and Japanese equities.
"We're getting closer to the end of this cycle," Manuell said. "There's a lot of enthusiasm, a lot of excitement."
"We're still very comfortable that international equities will outperform the Australian equity market."
AustralianSuper's balanced fund's biggest holdings included stakes in BHP, Commonwealth Bank, Nvidia, National Australia Bank and Rio Tinto as of December 31, according to its most recent disclosures. One-third of its assets were in international shares as of March 31, and one-quarter was invested in Australian shares.
Manuell replaced long-time AustralianSuper boss Mark Delaney this week after running the fund's hefty Australian equities book since 2013 and said he was excited to be responsible for a much broader investment portfolio.
He said addressing under-performance in AustralianSuper's infrastructure and property investment teams would be among his first major moves. Its private equity and private credit performance improved in FY26. AustralianSuper increased its reliance on third-party asset managers in both, he said.
The returns come in a year when Australia's S&P/ASX 200 notched a 2.77 per cent gain, about one-third of its long-term average. Australians had $4.5 trillion invested in superannuation funds as of March 30, according to industry association ASFA, about three-quarters of which is in industry, retail or large public-sector funds.
AustralianSuper is the largest fund by number of members and assets.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Anthony Macdonald
heading: Superannuation and Investments
